questions.stem: Slide 5 introduces the basic abstractions of access control. Which definition correctly describes a *subject*?

choices.text: A piece of data or a resource (e.g., a file or network packet).
An entity that wishes to access an object — typically a process executing on behalf of a user/principal.
A human end user.
A permission such as read or write.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: A subject is the *active* entity in the access-control triple (S × O × P). It is the program/process that initiates an access request on behalf of a principal.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Abstraction** — That is an *object*, the passive resource being accessed.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Abstraction** — A human is a *user*; once authenticated in a context, the user becomes a *principal*.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Abstraction** — Read/write are *rights* (permissions), not subjects.

questions.stem: Slide 7 shows Jian → {Jian.TS, Jian.S, Jian.C, Jian.U}. Why is the user-to-principal relation deliberately one-to-many?

choices.text: It enables accountability and least privilege — one user can act in distinct authenticated contexts (e.g., separate API keys, classification levels) without leaking authority across them.
Each principal is a separate person sharing the same account.
The OS forces every login to spawn four principals.
It makes the user database larger and therefore more secure.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 9 explicitly cites accountability and least privilege. By splitting the user into context-specific principals, compromise of one principal does not grant the attacker the user's full authority.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Concept** — Principals are *contexts of one user*, not separate humans.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Fabrication** — No fixed principal count is enforced.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Reason** — Database size is unrelated to least privilege.

questions.stem: What is the *defining* property of Discretionary Access Control?

choices.text: Access is determined by a system-wide policy that only an administrator can change.
Access rights can be propagated at the subject's discretion — possession of a right is sufficient to grant access.
Access is bound to roles which are then assigned to users.
Access requires a security label that dominates the object's classification.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 14: DAC 'allows access rights to be propagated at subject's discretion' and 'subjects can determine who has access to their objects.'

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Policy** — That describes MAC, not DAC.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Policy** — Label dominance is the BLP/MAC mechanism.

questions.stem: What does each cell M[s,o] of an access control matrix store?

choices.text: The set A ⊆ S × O × P represented as a single integer.
The set of permissions p ∈ P that subject s holds on object o.
The session token used by subject s.
A single permission shared by all subjects on object o.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slides 15–16: A ⊆ S × O × P; the matrix view stores at M[s,o] the subset of permissions granted to subject s on object o (e.g., {rw}, {kill}, {r}).

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Encoding** — Cells are sets of permissions, not packed integers.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concept** — Session tokens belong to authentication, not access matrices.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Resolution** — The whole point of the matrix is per-subject permissions.

questions.stem: How do ACLs and capabilities differ in *where* the authorization data lives?

choices.text: They are equivalent — both store data with the subject.
ACLs attach a (subject, permission) list to each *object*; capabilities give each *subject* tokens listing (object, permission) pairs.
ACLs live in the kernel; capabilities live in userspace applications.
ACLs require encryption; capabilities are always plaintext.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: ACLs are a column-wise slicing of the access matrix (per object); capabilities are a row-wise slicing (per subject). Same matrix, different physical storage.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Fabrication** — Placement is not the kernel/userspace distinction.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Reversed** — Capabilities typically need cryptographic unforgeability; ACLs don't require encryption.

questions.stem: Slide 19: ACLs require authentication of subjects. What do *capabilities* require instead?

choices.text: They require authentication of subjects, just by a different protocol.
Unforgeability and controlled propagation of capability tokens — typically achieved with cryptography.
Same-origin policy enforced by the OS.
A trusted directory service to map names to keys.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Capabilities are *bearer tokens*: holding the token = holding the right. Authentication of the holder is unnecessary; what matters is that tokens cannot be forged and propagation is controlled.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Direct Contradiction** — Slide 19 says capabilities do *not* require subject authentication.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Domain** — Same-origin policy is a browser concept.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Concept** — Trusted directories are a PKI primitive, not a capability mechanism.

questions.stem: Slide 22: Principal A runs a 'Good Program' that secretly contains Trojan code. The Trojan creates File G with ACL `B:r, A:w` and copies File F (where ACL is `A:r`) into G. What did the attacker accomplish?

choices.text: The Trojan elevates A to root.
Information from File F (which only A can read) is laundered into File G, where Principal B — who could not read F directly — can now read F's contents via G.
The Trojan changes File F's ACL to grant B direct read access.
The Trojan exploits a buffer overflow in the kernel.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: DAC happily lets the Trojan use A's read on F and write on G — both are A's legitimate rights. The leak is *authority abuse*, not a permissions violation. F's ACL is never modified.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Attack** — No privilege escalation is needed; the Trojan abuses A's existing rights.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Mechanism** — F's ACL stays the same; that's the point.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Class** — Trojan Horse is an authority-abuse attack, not memory corruption.

questions.stem: Slide 21 says DAC's underlying problem is 'a difference between trusting a person and trusting a program.' Why does this break DAC?

choices.text: DAC assumes all programs are written in memory-safe languages.
Programs run with the user's full authority, so any malicious or buggy program (e.g., a Trojan or exploited service) can exercise *all* of the user's rights against the user's interests.
DAC encrypts user data with a weak key.
DAC cannot be represented as an access matrix.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Subjects (programs) inherit the principal's full authority. The user trusts a coworker not to leak data, but cannot vouch for every program executing under their account — yet DAC grants those programs the user's full rights.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Concern** — Memory safety is not DAC's failure mode.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — DAC is naturally expressible as a matrix.

questions.stem: Which property distinguishes MAC from DAC?

choices.text: MAC uses an access control matrix; DAC does not.
In MAC, access is governed by a system-wide policy (typically based on security labels) changeable only by the administrator; subjects cannot grant their rights to others.
MAC permits subjects to propagate access at their discretion.
MAC is only used for integrity, never confidentiality.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 12 contrasts MAC ('system-wide policies … changed only by the administrator') with DAC's discretionary propagation. The non-discretionary, label-based nature is MAC's hallmark.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong** — Both DAC and MAC can be expressed via matrices.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Scope** — MAC covers both: BLP for confidentiality, Biba for integrity.

questions.stem: BLP's Simple Security Property (SS): *no read up*. What is its purpose?

choices.text: Prevent a low-cleared subject from reading information classified above its level.
Prevent a high-cleared subject from leaking information downward.
Prevent integrity corruption of high-classified data.
Enforce role separation.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: SS protects confidentiality on the read path: a Confidential-cleared subject cannot read a Top-Secret file, so high-classified information cannot enter low-cleared workflows via direct reads.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Property** — That's the goal of the Star property (no write down).
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Model** — BLP is confidentiality-only; integrity is Biba's domain.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Model** — Role separation is RBAC.

questions.stem: BLP's Star (*) Property says *no write down*. Why is this rule needed *in addition* to 'no read up'?

choices.text: To prevent buffer overflow when writing large files.
To stop a *Trojan Horse* running with high-level subject privileges from writing classified data into a lower-classified object readable by uncleared subjects.
Because writing is computationally more expensive than reading.
To enforce role separation across clearance levels.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 29 explicitly cites 'fear of Trojan Horse.' SS blocks direct reads, but a Trojan inside a TS-cleared subject could otherwise write TS data into a U-classified file and leak it. No-write-down closes that channel.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Reasoning** — Performance isn't the BLP rationale.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Model** — Role separation is RBAC.

questions.stem: Slide 32: subject s1 reads o1 (Secret) at L_c=Secret, then drops to L_c=Unclassified and writes o2 (Unclassified). Each individual state is 'secure' yet the system leaks. What fix does the slide propose?

choices.text: Disallow all writes after any read.
Subjects cannot drop their current level below the highest level read so far — i.e., no self-declassification.
Force every subject to operate at its maximal level only.
Encrypt all writes with the subject's private key.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The leak depends on s1 voluntarily lowering L_c after reading Secret. Forbidding that downgrade preserves BLP's invariant: information from level L cannot flow into a write at any level below L.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Too Restrictive** — Not the recommended fix; would break legitimate workflows.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Loses Flexibility** — Eliminates the benefit of separating current vs maximal level.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Mechanism** — Encryption doesn't prevent declassification by the subject.

questions.stem: Slide 33 shows two Trojan-infected subjects at TS and U with information flowing via 'timing of packets being sent.' Why does BLP's star-property fail to block this?

choices.text: Because timing is encrypted by default.
Because the star-property regulates only explicit read/write operations on labeled objects; the timing of legitimate operations forms a *covert channel* outside the reference monitor's scope.
Because the high subject has root privileges.
Because BLP doesn't apply to network traffic.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Covert channels exploit shared resources whose state is not itself a labeled object — CPU time, packet timing, cache occupancy. The reference monitor sees only authorized ops; the channel hides in their side effects.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Irrelevant** — Privilege level isn't the bypass mechanism.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong** — BLP applies system-wide; the issue is channel type, not medium.

questions.stem: The Biba integrity model is described as 'BLP upside down.' Which rule pair is correct for Biba?

choices.text: No read up, no write down.
No read down, no write up.
No reads or writes across levels at all.
Read freely, write only at your own level.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slides 34–35: Biba protects integrity by preventing high-integrity subjects from *reading low-integrity data* (which could corrupt their decisions) and from *writing high-integrity objects* (which could pollute them). It is BLP's dual.

distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Rule** — Reading down imports low-integrity data.

questions.stem: BLP and Biba look like duals. Why doesn't an OS simply 'AND' them together to get both confidentiality and integrity?

choices.text: They cannot be combined — the rules are mathematically inconsistent.
Combining 'no read up' (BLP) with 'no read down' (Biba) restricts subjects to reads at *their exact level only*; same for writes — the result is too restrictive for most real workflows.
Biba was deprecated in 1989.
BLP and Biba use incompatible label alphabets.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: BLP allows reads at-or-below; Biba allows reads at-or-above. Their intersection is reads at the same level only. Same for writes. Systems usually pick one or layer them carefully (e.g., Windows uses a Biba-like integrity model for malware containment).

distractorAnalysis.A: **False** — They are consistent, just very restrictive when combined.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Biba is still in use (e.g., Windows).
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — Both can use the same totally-ordered label set.

questions.stem: Chinese Wall *read* rule: subject S can read object O only if…

choices.text: O is in the same company dataset as some object S has previously read, OR O belongs to a conflict-of-interest class in which S has not yet read any object.
O is at a security level dominated by S's clearance.
O is owned by S.
O has no integrity label.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 38: once S reads from CD1 inside COI1, S is 'inside the wall' for CD1 and barred from CD2 in COI1. S can still read freely from any CD in another COI it hasn't touched.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concept** — Ownership is a DAC notion.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Model** — Chinese Wall doesn't use integrity labels.

questions.stem: Chinese Wall's *write* rule is stricter: S can write O only if S can read O AND S has never read an object O' with CD(O') ≠ CD(O). What attack does this rule prevent?

choices.text: Privilege escalation.
*Information laundering*: without the rule, S1 could read CD1 and write into CD3, where S2 (who has read CD2) could then read it — combining CD1 and CD2 conflict-of-interest data indirectly.
BLP no-write-down violations.
Encryption-key reuse.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 39's scenario: S1 reads CD1 and writes O6 in CD3; S2 reads CD2 then reads O6 — S2 has effectively combined CD1+CD2. The strict write rule says: once you've read across CDs, you can never write again, breaking the laundering channel.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Concern** — Privilege escalation isn't a conflict-of-interest issue.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Model** — BLP and Chinese Wall are independent policies.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Irrelevant** — Encryption is unrelated.

questions.stem: What is the primary motivation for RBAC (1995) over DAC/MAC?

choices.text: RBAC eliminates the need for authentication.
Real-world security policies are dynamic — users change jobs and projects. RBAC factors permissions through roles so admins reassign by changing role membership rather than rewriting per-user grants.
RBAC is the only model that supports encryption.
RBAC removes the need for an access control matrix.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 41: when a user is promoted, their rights must change. Per-user grants don't scale; RBAC adds a role layer so a single membership change updates all derived permissions.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Irrelevant** — Encryption is orthogonal to access control.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — RBAC still uses matrices internally; it adds indirection through roles.

questions.stem: Slide 42 says RBAC can be configured to do DAC *or* MAC. How does it simulate MAC?

choices.text: By treating roles as security clearances and assigning users to clearance roles (ESORICS 96).
By disabling all roles entirely.
By using JavaScript instead of Liquid templates.
By making every role its own conflict-of-interest class.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 42: 'roles simulate clearances (ESORICS 96).' A role hierarchy mirrors a clearance lattice, with role inheritance encoding dominance.

distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Model** — Conflict-of-interest is Chinese Wall.

questions.stem: Which of the following is *NOT* listed as an RBAC shortcoming on slide 45?

choices.text: Role granularity is often inadequate, leading to *role explosion*.
Role design and engineering is difficult and expensive.
RBAC requires more cryptography than DAC.
Adjusting roles based on local/global situational factors is difficult.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 45 lists role explosion, expensive role engineering, cumbersome user/permission assignment, and difficulty handling situational factors. Cryptography is *not* mentioned — it's orthogonal to RBAC.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Listed** — Role explosion is the canonical RBAC complaint.
distractorAnalysis.B: **Listed** — Role engineering cost is on slide 45.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Listed** — Local/global adjustment difficulty is on slide 45.
